MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Plaintiffs Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Adjudication Of Defendants Second And Seventh Affirmative Defenses [C.C.P. 437c]
Off Calendar Nov-23-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. JOSEPH S. MAY, TEL. NO. 1-415-781-3333 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. BRIAN GEARINGER OF THE GEARINGER LAW GROUP, TEL. NO. 1-415-440-3175 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. DAVID J. SEPANIK OF THE O'MELVERNY & MYERS LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-477-4511 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANTS.

RULING - A R G U E D; PLAINTIFF ABBEY DYKHOUSE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND Motion For Summary Adjudication Of Defendants Second And Seventh Affirmative Defenses [C.C.P. 437c] - The court grants plaintiff's motion for summary adjudication. The statute of limitations defense and release defense do not bar plaintiff's claims as a matter of law. The statute of limitations defense does not bar plaintiff's claims. The republication rule applies. First, the evidence establishes that the photographs were uploaded to defendant's new products. (D's supplemental special rog. response 2); Cuccioli v. Jekyll & Hyde Neue Metropol Bremem Theater Produktion GmbH & Co. (S.D.N.Y. 7/2/01) 150 F.Supp.2d 566, 573. Second, one "general rule" is that a statement is republished when it is "repeat[ed] or recirculate[d] ... to a new audience." Hebrew Acad. of S.F. v. Goldman, (2007) 42 Cal.4th 883, 891. Here, defendant admits that the photographs were used at its new Brazilian store and the library of photographs were modified to be understandable in the new market. Defendant's intentional reaching out to new customers constitutes a republishing. The e-mail release does not bar plaintiff's claims. First, a reading of the e-mail chain indicates that plaintiff agreed to allow the use of photographs of Stitch. Second, even if the release were as broad as defendant contends, the written general release superseded the e-mail release. The e-mail chain expressly contemplates that the parties will enter into a written release that will govern the parties' conduct. CC 1625 provides: "The execution of a contract in writing, whether the law requires it to be written or not, supersedes all the negotiations or stipulations concerning its matter which preceded or accompanied the execution of the instrument." Defendant's argument that the e-mail release and general release are separate agreements and that they can both exist because the general release is not fully integrated lacks merit. The e-mail exchange indicates that the general release will control.
The general release does not bar plaintiff's claims. First, based on the e-mail release analysis, the release was supposed to apply only to photographs of Stitch. Second, even if the release were as broad as defendant contends, defendant was only allowed to use plaintiff's photographs "for the period of one year." The undisputed evidence demonstrates that defendant used the photographs outside of that one year period. Defendant's incorporation argument is a misreading of the general release. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter : Melanie Gheno, CSR # 7489 =302/ehg
